title: practical english usage - Part 69
content:
You could have told me you were getting married.

behaviour.

Could/might have... + past participle can be used in criticisms about past You might try to be nicer to my mother.

You could ask before you borrow my car.

Could and might can be used to criticise people for not doing things.

3 criticisms:      You     could      have      told     me    . . .

Shall we go out for a meal?  Let's go and see Lucy, shall we?

What on earth shall we do?

What time shall we come and see you?

Shall I carry your bag?

Shall I open a window? (     Will I open a window?) American English, this is rather formal.  Will is not used in this way.

for instructions or decisions, to offer services, and to make suggestions. In Questions with shall I/we are used (in both British and American English) to ask 2 asking     for   instructions         and   decisions:      shall For more about if... will, ►    243.

You might see if John's free this evening.

If you don't know what to do, you could sort out your photos.

When you've finished the dishes you could iron the clothes, if you like.

Could and might are often used in suggestions.

Do you think you could help me for a few minutes?

Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs grammar «81     permission: can, could, may, might, be allowed to not She's always been allowed to do what she liked, (     She's always eeuld...) expressed in other ways: for example with forms of be allowed to.

Modal  verbs have no infinitives or participles. When  necessary, permission is 5 be   allowed         to Mr Newson agreed that I might look round, (very formal) The manager says we may leave our coats in the waiting room.

She says we can park here. They told us we couldn't go in yet.

verbs, and could/might after past verbs.

permission (general or particular). Can!may are used after present reporting Can, could, may and might can however all be used to report the giving of 4 indirect      speech difference between could and was/were able to. ►    82.4.) (The difference between could and was!were allowed to is similar to the Daniel couldn't watch TV yesterday because he was naughty.

But could not can be used to talk about one particular action.

(     . .. l)amel c^tkhu^atch-TV for an hour.) Yesterday evening Daniel was allowed to watch TV for an hour.

When I was a child, I could watch TV whenever I wanted to.

use could to talk about permission for one particular action in the past.

permission to do something at any time ('general permission'), but we  do not In talking about the past, we  use could (but not might) to say that somebody had 3  past    permission:       could      is  not   always     possible not Can everybody park here? (     May&wybodypMk-ht*rt#) this way.

and about things that are allowed by rules or laws. May is not normally used in Can is used to talk about permission that has already been given or refused, 2 talking     about     permission:       Can   everybody           . . . ?

than can't /may not.

Must not (►  73.3) can also be used to refuse permission; it is more emphatic uses, but this does not reflect actual usage.

There is an old belief that may!might are more 'correct' than can/could in these 'May I smoke? 'I'm sorry, you may not.' May not (but not might not} can be used to refuse permission.

grammar •  82  ability: can and could I managed to find the street, but I couldn't find her house.

occasion.

However, we  use couldn't to say that something did not happen on one not I found a really nice dress in the sale, (     l eəuldfind.. .) noi (     .. .-iee-emild-geL-hHhe-top...) After six hours' climbing we succeeded in getting to the top of the mountain.

How many eggs were you able to get? (     . .. eeuldyeu get?) yesterday...) not 1 managed to run 10km yesterday in under an hour, (     L-emM-runLQIem occasion. Instead, we  use other expressions.

We   do not normally use could to say that somebody did something on one When I was younger, I could run 10km in under 40 minutes.

something at any time, whenever he/she wanted.

We   use could for ‘ general ability' - for example to say that somebody could do 4 past:   could      is  not   always     possible She was able to read when she was four.
